original_abstract:
*Prangos ferulacea* is a highly valued medicinal plant in Iran, prized for its unique therapeutic properties and distinctive flavor. This study aimed to characterize the chemical profile and biological potential of its aqueous extract. Chemical analysis using High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) identified p-coumaric acid as the primary constituent, while Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) confirmed the prevalence of functional groups associated with phenolics, flavonoids, tannins, and carboxylic acids such as caffeic and coumaric acid. The extract yielded a total phenolic content (TPC) of 202.04 ± 5.46 mg gallic acid equivalent (GAE)/g dry weight, a total flavonoid content (TFC) of 1,909.46 ± 13 μg quercetin (QE)/g dry weight, and a beta-carotene level of 2.91 mg/100 g.

The extract demonstrated robust antioxidant activity across multiple assays, with IC50 values of 274 ± 7.2 μg/mL for DPPH and 120.45 ± 9.6 μg/mL for ABTS radical scavenging, alongside a FRAP value of 1.92 ± 0.05 μg ascorbic acid equivalent (AAE)/g. Cytotoxicity testing against HT-29 cancer cells revealed significant anti-proliferative effects (IC50 = 82.15 ± 0.02 μg/mL). Furthermore, antimicrobial screening—utilizing disk and well diffusion, as well as MIC and MBC determinations—highlighted *Listeria monocytogenes* as the most susceptible pathogen (MIC: 16 mg/mL; MBC: 128 mg/mL). Microscopic analysis via scanning electron (SEM) and confocal laser scanning (CLSM) confirmed substantial morphological damage and cellular disruption in treated *L. monocytogenes*. Molecular analysis further revealed that the extract significantly downregulated the expression of genes essential for biofilm formation. Collectively, these results validate the practical potential of *P. ferulacea* as a potent source of natural antioxidants, anticancer agents, and antimicrobial compounds.
humanized_abstract:
Prangos ferulacea is an important medicinal plant in Iran because of its special medicinal and pleasant taste. This study investigated the composition and the biological activities of aqueous extract of P. ferulacea. By HPLC analysis, p-coumaric acid was confirmed as the main constituents of the aqueous extract of P. ferulacea. Also, FTIR spectrum indicated the existence of functional groups characteristic of phenolic compounds, flavonoids, tannins and carboxylic acids such as caffeic and coumaric acid. The total phenolic content (TPC) of aqueous extract of P. ferulacea was found to be 202.04 ± 5.46 mg gallic acid equivalent (GAE)/g dry weight, the total flavonoid content (TFC) was 1909.46 ± 13 μg quercetin (QE)/g dry weight and beta-carotene content was 2.91 mg/100 g.

The extract was assessed in vitro for antioxidant activity using DPPH and ABTS assays, which produced IC50 values of 274 ± 7.2 μg/mL and 120.45 ± 9.6 μg/mL respectively. The FRAP assay also produced a value of 1.92 ± 0.05 μg AAE/g of extract. Cytotoxic effects against the HT-29 colorectal cancer cell line were assessed and IC50 value of 82.15 ± 0.02 μg/mL obtained. Antimicrobial screening was performed using disk diffusion and well diffusion assays, while MIC and MBC values were determined and the results showed that *Listeria monocytogenes* was the most sensitive strain (MIC value of 16 mg/mL, and MBC value of 128 mg/mL). The effect of extract on *L. monocytgenes* was also visualized using SEM and CLSM, indicating a significant structural alteration and damage at the cellular level. *P. ferulacea* extract led to a down-regulation of the genes responsible for biofilm formation. Hence *P. ferulacea* extract has the potential for use as a natural product for the control and/or prevention of cancer, as well as for use as an antioxidant and antimicrobial agent.
